Hmm, the problem with this is it reintroduces a bug I had earlier. To implement this, the module would have to fetch the events using the API on every refresh. The problem is, if this is done quickly, py3status can crash. 

It's hard to debug because it only happens when the module (and its events) are refreshed with a bunch of quick button presses. For example, if I set button 2 to refresh the module and all the events, if I press button 2 over and over very quickly, the module will eventually crash. There seems to be some problem when some events are being fetched, and the process is interrupted to call `_get_events()` again. If this is done fast enough, the `events` dict contains garbage, and that takes down the module. Unfortunately, there's nothing in the py3status log that's helpful in figuring it out.

That's why I have that `self.no_update` boolean. I use it to make sure we don't fetch events until a button event is handled, so that an event update cannot be interrupted. This prevents the crash from happening.
Done, now it is possible to set `self.format_notification = None` to disable notification. I intentionally implemented as least as possible functionality to keep this PR focused on its original goal.

If a test takes 30 seconds, it's not a unit test but rather an integration test and should then not rely on randon data. 

The majority of our tests run in less than a second and we should design the default values for these cases. We employ the same concept for most of our automation scripts - for example, it's sufficient to run CI/build.py -p armv7 and that's it. The default behaviour should be the main use cases and everything specialized (like a long running test) should require an additional Parameter. In general, it's best practice to keep the number of configuration options for the standard case to an absolute minimum.

Both this and the optparse code have a minor issue with `--ignore_dir`. They both use a mutable default (`setup.add_argument('--ignore_dir', default=['.git', '.svn', ...])`). That's used as-is when parsing arguments, so supplying `--ignore_dir` mutates it, thus changing the default for the *next* time arguments are parsed. 

Test cases almost always wind up parsing arguments twice, once for the defaults, once for the invocation-specific arguments.

In practice, this doesn't seem to be a problem.

I have been playing with the API and discovered the following things:

projectId can take either just a projectId or a list of projectIds. The docs say it should take a list.

If you don't give it a list, both = and : operator will work, but : operator will return an extraneous token, that if used will keep returning just itself.

If you do give it a list ,only = will work, : operator will return 0 results with a nextPageToken that keeps returning itself.

I am following up internally about these issues but I think even if these bugs are fixed, this is the correct change to make since it's indexed.

Recommend in general adding style-specific class names rather than using html-based selectors. This supports safer template refactoring.

For example, if someone comes along in the future and edits the template to e.g. remove `submit` or (gasp) not change `button` to `div`, they might not realize that will remove some style targeted at it. However if it has a class associated with it, they know to check the additional styles below.

I'm actually not sure we can do this safely.  I think my original comment about requiring `tensor.dim() == mask.dim()` only until pytorch 0.2 was wrong, and we probably should just leave this as it was (except we can probably remove the `expand_as`).

Consider:

If this is for an embedding, we have tensor with shape `(batch_size, num_tokens, embedding_dim)`, with mask `(batch_size, num_tokens)`.  We want to unsqueeze the last dimension, and that's fine.  But in BiDAF, I use this with a tensor of shape `(batch_size, passage_length, question_length)`, with a mask of shape `(batch_size, question_length)`.  Unsqueezing the last dimension here is wrong, and could lead to odd and unpredictable behavior of `passage_length` happens to equal `question_length` - the mask will be applied on the wrong dimension.  I think we just need to require the caller to have put the mask into the same dimension as the tensor, so we don't have to guess in this function.

This change was done to fix tests logic. We expect nodes to be in following order: 
`Alpha, Beta, Gamma, Delta`
as they are in Greek alphabet, but nodes sort their remotes by name during primary selection and the order changes: 
`Alpha, Beta, Delta, Gamma`.

This causes following problems:

1. If you want to stop non primary you stop last one, which you expect to be Delta - but it is actually Gamma. And if you then start a view change you get unexpected behaviour - instance 1 decides Delta to be next primary, but Delta is down.

2. It confuses you when you read test logs

We have another test `test_node_catchup_after_disconnect.py` to check catch-up after a node is disconnected.
This test doesn't perform disconnect intentionally. It just removes A node from the Loop, so that a Node receives messages, but don't process them.

Nodes sort their remotes by **rank** and **not name**. For old style nodes, rank is generated on the basis of name and for new style nodes, it's generated on the basis of order in ledger
You can use `nodes_by_rank` and it will give you correct node for both old style and new style nodes.
